free_text: 16O(alpha,gamma)20Ne S factor: Measurements and R-
matrix analysis
main reference, normalized data
original gas target data, Stuttgart,
second reference
Experiment performed at the Nuclear
Science Laboratory at the University of Notre Dame,
using the 3.7MV KN Van de Graaff accelerator.
Experiment performed at the Dynamitron
accelerator laboratory of the University of Stuttgart,
using windowless recirculating gas target RHINOCEROS
Used at Notre Dame, described in the main reference.
Solid oxygen Ta2O5 sample, produced by anodizing a
0.25 mm thick Ta backing using 99.999% enriched 16-O
water. Typical thickness of the tantalum-oxide,
Ta2O5, layer was around 20 mu-g/cm2, corresponding
to 10 keV energy loss for E(alpha) = 2.5 MeV.
Gamma-ray detector setup used at Notre
Dame,  described in the main reference.
The gamma ray detection system consisted
of four NaI(Tl) detectors and a high-purity germanium
clover detector, with the clover operated in add-back
mode. The clover detector was placed at a distance of
0.65 cm from the target, and at 45deg with respect to
the beam direction.
Method used at Notre Dame experiment.
To reduce background, NaI and HPGe
detectors were operated in coincidence mode with the
NaI(Tl)'s observing the high-energy primary gamma-ray
decay of the 1.3 MeV resonance state, and the clover
observing the secondary 1.634 MeV gamma-ray transition
from the first excited state to the ground state.
Compiled by S.H.
